sentences.text: When the bunker-- it-- and then in the summer, we had to stock up from summer the food because in the winter was harder to get food yet because if you walked on the snow it would show prints, and then they could follow in there.
So all summer long, whatever we could do, we'd stock up for the winter.
Yeah.
It was not too bad and the bunker, no.
It's very deep in the ground.
We'd have something, like, to cook or whatever, but you couldn't get out too much-- what do you call it-- smoke from the stove.
You know what I think they do is most of the time they did it there in the day, and at night it would show up more.
It took us about half a year because must of them were on a train.
And the Russians, when they come over, they had the only guns.
And since that's where they're going to make a they thought they're going to be getting a couple Germans, we'd get a couple more guns.
But later on, we did find some of them.
And some of them are other-- other groups were fighting with them, and they got rid of a couple of them.
And they left their guns in the woods, and we kept picked them up.
We did not name.
Huh?
Not exact.
No.
It was-- everybody was, like, together.
You know what I mean?
I realize-- I don't remember.
I don't think there was strictly a guy that we were asking most of the time for anything they want to do as volunteers.
Sometimes.
Not all the time.
Everybody did go, you know, because there was a very dangerous mission.
You volunteer.
But then the next time you said, well, I've been here twice.
Why don't you get him once?
And I mean, everybody, even if you don't want it, you had to do it though.
Well, that's the close call I had.
Nothing.
You were cut off from the world.
You only-- a lot of-- some of them could escape into town and check up on things what goes on.
And not-- the Gentile could do it though.
No, to Miedzyrzecz because they are Gentile.
You know, they recognized who was-- who was Jewish and who not.
The Poles, no.
There were no Poles altogether.
And one time, we had, like, two of them.
And they did go out for some mission, and it didn't come through.
So they think maybe they turned them in.
Well, they said to them, they don't want them.
You have 48 hours.
Find yourself another group.
I mean, they didn't punish them or anything.
They were-- didn't enough proof.
But they tell them, there's other groups over here.
Probably there was more than one, you know.
So they tell them to hook up with another group.
Yeah.
Just a plain gun, a single bullet.
Like you're here.
Over here you've got-- what do you call it?
Hunting-- hunting-- what do you call it?
A single bullet gun, you pull the- the trigger in it.
I don't know.
We-- we had-- we thought we did, but I didn't go back to check out.
I think we on one mission was successful.
We could pick up a couple of guns with that.
And later, I hear that it-- that it was so many.
But it was hard to prove it [INAUDIBLE].
The hardest thing is the sickness and food because I remember over there towards the end of the war, I got dysentery, or something like that.
And before I know, the next guy got it and the next guy.
And there was no medicine out to treat it.
That was the hardest thing though.
And a lot of them died because of that.
Yes.
Yeah.
Sure.
Yeah.
I think, I mean, it was a couple-- a couple of Jewish people, a boy and a man.
And I talked to them, and then I turned around, and they are gone.
A couple of them, at least four or five.
And you just didn't never-- I don't know.
It turned bad.
No-- and they didn't have no medicine out to control it.
But somehow, I don't know, I made it.
Don't ask me how or what.
I probably-- maybe-- maybe I didn't eat much.
And they were hungry, and they-- I don't know.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's like everything.
It's like they say in Jewish, it's [NON-ENGLISH].
You don't know.
I was not any better than the rest of them.
I know that much.
But that's the way it's supposed to be.
What can I tell you.
Well, like I tell you, you're in there, and then some of them-- some of them, they were not your-- OK Russian and did go out.
And they said they hear that the Russians are moving.
And they say there's so many kilometers.
And we got to hold out till they came.
Then things will be at least a little better.
You know, like now food is the main-- the main thing.
And then they come and say, well, they are 200 miles, or whatever, 200 kilometers.
In the next three days, they should be in our area.
Yeah, it's all you talk about.
You get everybody-- you have, like, a bread, and you slice it up.
And everybody gets-- and get that piece of bread.
And then the other guy turns around, and he calls the name.
So everybody get the equal parts in that.
You know what I mean?
Everybody turns around.
He says, well, this man, this man, everybody get it.
And then they have a little soup and that and-- that will do it.
Soup and bread, that's about it sometimes.
One day was better than the other.
Well, sometime they could catch some animal there in the woods.
But there was-- they were afraid to shoot there.
You know what I mean?
When you're shoot a hollow thing, you can hear it too.
If you were going in the woods and shoot, you can hear a big fire there.
And when you are hiding, you don't want the enemy to know where you're hiding.
A couple of times, the Germans tried to get in with the dogs.
And they went so far, and they couldn't see nothing.
And they turned right back.
But two, three times they tried, with a Ukrainian and everything.
But then, they were afraid to go in because most of the time when they got in too deep, they didn't get out anyway.
If-- you know, they just got-- they-- they killed them.
So I didn't want to go in too deep.
At what?
What?
Yeah.
At some point-- at some point where you know you-- you lost everybody and that your life isn't worth nothing anyway.
You know what I'm trying to say?
You don't have much.
You only have the-- the couple of people that you know about.
Beside that, you just-- you just don't have nobody.
It was hard even in, like, in when the Germans come in and you-- you walk on the sidewalk, and he said to me, you there!
We want you to walk on the-- on the street.
Yeah.
They don't let you walk on the-- on the sidewalk any--
Who me?
Here and there, yeah.
I had--
Oh, nothing, just manual jobs.
I remember one time in their winter, they got stuck, and then they couldn't bring nothing in or out.
You had to shovel out all this.
They didn't have the modern equipment.
I had to go and shovel snow.
I worked six hours there.
It was not only me, but others.
It was blowing around like your house so deep.
They had to take and shovel out the snow to make-- to bring in supplies or whatever.
But they supposed to pay you though.
But then they-- they paid some but very little though.
Yeah.
But then, yeah--
Well, you know, when you have-- when you hook up with a group and one-- and once you ever-- they-- how do you say?
Gave a lot of fire where you'd be wanting [INAUDIBLE].
And he needs-- tells to the other one, listen, we made it so far.
We're going to make it regardless of what.
What-- what if you were dead?
They didn't have a chance altogether.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
No.
[CRYING] No, I didn't think so.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
In what?
Like?
The hardest thing was being separated from the parents.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that's number one.
But then you had to fight for survival was number two.
And then when I get sick was number three.
I put this on there last.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Yeah, was-- was-- and as soon the ration come in, sort of, and they give me-- what do you call it--
Medical care.
In '43 they came in. '43? '40-- '43?
Know '44?
It's not so clear in my mind.
I think it's '40-- '44, in the beginning of '44 they were.
Yeah, '44.
In the beginning of-- in February of '44, they came in.
They went over to the town.
And then, we could hear-- we could hear a lot of rumbling-- you know the tanks and that.
And so OK, we send out a group of people.
And find out what happened.
And he says, forget it.
It's all over.
You cannot go at least wherever you want to go.
But I didn't stop during the time altogether.
The time when I was born, I didn't because I-- I find out there's nothing in there.
There's not-- nobody alive, so we're not going to stop there.
I-- I needed medical attention.
And then, right away they give me that medical attention.
Field hospital.
There for quite a bit.
I couldn't-- they-- I had-- we did-- they didn't have nothing neither.
They told the lungs filled up with-- they had to poke me with some needles and get-- jars like that.
What do you call it, pleurisy or something.
The fluid out.
And there was no medicine to dry it up so I had to stay there for six weeks.
And then after it was all done, you just-- you're too weak though.
But I will keep moving with them because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] now, we did find out that in, like, in Poland, these groups of Jewish Haganah, I guess.
And they going to help us to resettle or whatever.
But they are afraid to operate in Russia because the Russians don't let-- they call them the Zionists.
And they would catch them.
They just going to put them away to Siberia.
He says, whatever you guys want to do.
You want to stay here, you stay here.
But if you want to go, we're going to find you a place-- not in Poland whatever, but in Israel.
In the-- in Poland.
I was in Szczecin then.
By that time they-- they kicked out the Germans.
I think that was [NON-ENGLISH].
Szczecin, yeah.
It used to be the Germans, and then they come in.
And then they kicked them out.
And they gave it-- and it still was-- years ago, it was Poland, OK?
Then the Germans took it away.
And then they-- after the-- the war was over, they kicked them back out, and Germany took them all in.
Yeah, but after a certain point, then the Russian and the Polish were the same.
But you couldn't operate.
Like, if you were a Zionist or whatever under Russian territory, you couldn't operate and say you-- you were a Zionist.
A Zionist to them is like-- like you're a spy for them-- for them or something.
So they told us after--
Yeah.
Not too long.
No.
That's it.
I was feeling good.
I didn't need them then.
We were staying in there.
But then they organized groups of people, youth, and they wanted to bring to Israel.
I think it was about six of us.
We were six, and we were together.
And every day, we stayed together.
There was no-- no women.
They are all in Israel.
No.
I don't remember all of it.
I don't know.
I'm thinking it, but it don't come through that clear.
Go to Israel.
I mean, they set us up.
The Haganah.
They said, you're going to stay here till we have another place we're going to move you.
Just stay here.
We're going to feed you and everything.
And you don't have to do nothing, which they did.
And from there, from Szczecin, we went to I think Czechoslovakia.
And there, we stayed.
Well, there-- there was better.
Every time we made a move, was that life turned better a little bit.
In Czechoslovakia, we-- we stayed in a camp-- I forgot, I can't remember-- where the Jewish people got, you know--
It was not a-- a kibbutz, not a DP camp.
It was a concentration camp, and they let us stay there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they got to where they-- sure it wasn't camp?
I can't say.
But anyway, in Czechoslovakia, they helped us out.
We stayed-- we stayed until they were ready for us in-- in Germany.
And then they transported us back there.
And I told you.
Bad Reichenhall.
From Czechoslovakia to Bad Reichenhall.
No, I'm first in the kibbutz.
Though I don't figure the kibbutz because we didn't stay there too long.
I feel I was at Bad Reichenhall because most of the time, from 1946 to '49, I spent there most of the time.
Yeah, life was not too bad.
You were getting from UNRRA, you were getting a package every-- you know what I mean?
You're getting a package.
And a lot of packages come from the United States for the sick people.
Oh, yeah.
This was a-- a military camp.
And it's a German military camp.
And then they kicked them out, and they turned it into a DP camp.
And every family had one room only.
I think we had one room.
And we split it up in half.
It was me and my brother.
And the other was-- was five in one room.
And you have a-- in the basement you have a kitchen.
And-- and everybody eats there.
I mean, you're single-- all the single people eat in the mess.
And probably there were some people helping us out to leave the food in there.
Some used to make some for Shabbos, you know.
Although, most people didn't have nothing so that buyed whatever it is, and then they make it.
I remember they made all kind of stuff for us for the Shabbos.
But over there, I was less religious than I was home.
I don't know.
Things changed very much.
Yeah, I was in the police.
They needed somebody.
Well, I mean I-- when you think maybe the police, like in the ghetto, we didn't-- I didn't-- you didn't have no power.
People are well, all you have to do is have order, you know.
If you say a man and a wife, they have a fight, or you or someone have to stop them.
And you have to watch, like, contraband not to get in there, whatever.
There's some people they like to, you know, they bring in, like, cigarettes or something.
They bring them in anyway.
So that you stay by the door.
And they cut a hole by the fence, and they get through.
No.
I was too lenient, and I got bawled out a lot of times.
So they put me in a place where nobody was around there, you know.
At the back door, there was nobody there.
It's quiet.
You know, they checked up in there.
I suppose there's some-- some woman was crying in there.
I looked up.
She shouldn't have it-- whatever.
And I say, OK, go.
So someone turned me in though.
So he said, instead of firing you from the job, they are going to give you-- they are going to put you always in the back over there.
So this way, you wouldn't have no problem.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When, when you were getting extra money for that?
By doing that?
Yeah, I would.
I got fired.
I told them to chuck it.
Well, I think I'd gotten $25 a week or something.
And maybe-- that's about it, I guess.
Yeah. $25 or $30?
I don't remember.
Now, a lot of people, when they got in the camps, got married right away.
Hello, how are you?
And the huppah.
But you didn't.
No, no, no, no, no.
Not till-- what?
I was kind of young.
I didn't I find nobody.
There was not too many women.
Do you ever realize that?
Anyone that-- where the girls there was available, the older men picked them.
[LAUGHING] Yeah, I remember that.
When there was young women and the older men grabbed them the younger women.
I didn't know much better.
Yeah, OK.
And I remember most of the women were maybe 23 or 25.
And the men were like 30.
And they-- they probably know what was going on than me at that time.
Where?
No.
Uh-uh.
Bad Reichenhall, I come back here.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I come back to Appleton, Wisconsin.
They sponsored me.
The Jewish-- what do you call it?
The Jewish-- UNRRA or--
Joint Distribution Committee.
Who did you come to here?
I told you.
Didn't I tell you how I find a relative here?
I come to Appleton, and I stayed about three months.
Or two months.
The-- the-- Joint Distribution Committee says go over there.
And they-- they'll find a job for me.
Shovel coal.
[LAUGHS] In a-- in a stock room there.
But I didn't know it.
You know that they-- they asked me, why were you-- you know, you come out of the job.
You're all black.
And they all asked me, you like it?
I say, yeah.
How's the job?
The job-- you know, as a young man I used to work hard.
Is the job hard?
No.
Then the men-- the men from the store said, you know what?
You're going to know a little more English.
I have a better job for you.
This is a starting job.
I want to see if-- how good you're going to do with that.
And then he says we're going to move you up.
You can be a salesman or something.
And I says good.
And the main thing there was, I'm not a Jewish guy.
He's a refugee.
He's younger than me.
So he said to me, you know what?
We're going to go to Milwaukee.
Do you want to, I mean, do you want to go with us?
He had a car.
I didn't have a car.
I didn't have a car till 1955.
I figured I don't have no money.
I don't need a car.
And he had a sweet car.
But I mean, I bought a car when I had cash stored.
And I worked hard, and I had enough money.
But I bought a car.
To make the story short, he took me to Milwaukee.
And then, when you are a refugee, you'll come-- there's four people who says, what city are you?
And then one asked the other one, what city are you?
And you say you are from a town like that.
Do you know anybody from my town?
I mean, how are you going-- there's no communication.
There is no telephone.
So you would-- by the people, between the people, you communicate and find out what town they are, or who is alive now of what and everything.
That's why-- or you put your name in wherever you go.
You put your name, and people, you know, at certain places like in the kibbutz, or some other kibbutz.
And they check.
And then you come, the first thing you check to see-- who is alive.
And that's-- what did I say now?
I forgot.
